Article.ArticleTitle: Different latent class models were used and evaluated for assessing the accuracy of campylobacter diagnostic tests: overcoming imperfect reference standards?

Abstract.AbstractText: In the absence of perfect reference standard, classical techniques result in biased diagnostic accuracy and prevalence estimates. By statistically defining the true disease status, latent class models (LCM) constitute a promising alternative. However, LCM is a complex method which relies on parametric assumptions, including usually a conditional independence between tests and might suffer from data sparseness. We carefully applied LCMs to assess new campylobacter infection detection tests for which bacteriological culture is an imperfect reference standard. Five diagnostic tests (culture, polymerase chain reaction and three immunoenzymatic tests) of campylobacter infection were collected in 623 patients from Bordeaux and Lyon Hospitals, France. Their diagnostic accuracy were estimated with standard and extended LCMs with a thorough examination of models goodness-of-fit. The model including a residual dependence specific to the immunoenzymatic tests best complied with LCM assumptions. Asymptotic results of goodness-of-fit statistics were substantially impaired by data sparseness and empirical distributions were preferred. Results confirmed moderate sensitivity of the culture and high performances of immunoenzymatic tests. LCMs can be used to estimate diagnostic tests accuracy in the absence of perfect reference standard. However, their implementation and assessment require specific attention due to data sparseness and limitations of existing software.

Reference.Citation: Bossuyt PM et al. (2016) STARD 2015: updated reporting guidelines for all diagnostic accuracy studies. Annals of Translational Medicine 4, 85.
Valenstein PN (1990) Evaluating diagnostic tests with imperfect standards. American Journal of Clinical Pathology 93, 252–258.
Ihorst G et al. (2007) The use of imperfect diagnostic tests had an impact on prevalence estimation. Journal of Clinical Epidemiology 60, 902–910.
Rutjes AWS et al. (2007) Evaluation of diagnostic tests when there is no gold standard. A review of methods. Health Technology Assessment 11, iii, ix-51.
Reitsma JB et al. (2009) A review of solutions for diagnostic accuracy studies with an imperfect or missing reference standard. Journal of Clinical Epidemiology 62, 797–806.
Walter SD et al. (2012) Effect of dependent errors in the assessment of diagnostic or screening test accuracy when the reference standard is imperfect. Statistics in Medicine 31, 1129–1138.
Miller WC (2012) Commentary: reference-test bias in diagnostic-test evaluation a problem for epidemiologists, too. Epidemiology 23, 83–85.
Hui SL and Walter SD (1980) Estimating the error rates of diagnostic tests. Biometrics 36, 167–171.
Walter SD and Irwig LM (1988) Estimation of test error rates, disease prevalence and relative risk from misclassified data: a review. Journal of Clinical Epidemiology 41, 923–937.
Qu Y, Tan M and Kutner MH (1996) Random effects models in latent class analysis for evaluating accuracy of diagnostic tests. Biometrics 52, 797–810.
Hui SL and Zhou XH (1998) Evaluation of diagnostic tests without gold standards. Statistical Methods in Medical Research 7, 354–370.
Goetghebeur E et al. (2000) Diagnostic test analyses in search of their gold standard: latent class analyses with random effects. Statistical Methods in Medical Research 9, 231–248.
Albert PS (2009) Estimating diagnostic accuracy of multiple binary tests with an imperfect reference standard. Statistics in Medicine 28, 780–797.
van Smeden M et al. (2013) Latent class models in diagnostic studies when there is no reference standard - a systematic review. American Journal of Epidemiology 179, 423–431.
Collins J and Huynh M (2014) Estimation of diagnostic test accuracy without full verification: a review of latent class methods. Statistics in Medicine 33, 4141–4169.

Hadgu A, Dendukuri N and Hilden J (2005) Evaluation of nucleic acid amplification tests in the absence of a perfect gold-standard test. A review of the statistical and epidemiologic issues. Epidemiology 16, 604–612.
Torrance-Rynard VL and Walter SD (1997) Effects of dependent errors in the assessment of diagnostic test performance. Statistics in Medicine 16, 2157–2175.
Alonzo TA and Pepe MS (1999) Using a combination of reference tests to assess the accuracy of a new diagnostic test. Statistics in Medicine 18, 2987–3003.
Albert PS and Dodd LE (2004) A cautionary note on the robustness of latent class models for estimating diagnostic error without a gold standard. Biometrics 60, 427–435.
Pepe MS and Janes H (2006) Insights into latent class analysis of diagnostic test performance. Biostatistics (Oxford, England) 8, 474–484.
Menten J, Boelaert M and Lesaffre E (2008) Bayesian latent class models with conditionally dependent diagnostic tests: a case study. Statistics in Medicine 27, 4469–4488.
Reiser M and Lin Y (1999) A goodness-of-fit test for the latent class model when expected frequencies are small. Sociological Methodology 29, 81–111.
Formann AK (2003) Latent class model diagnostics—a review and some proposals. Computational Statistics and Data Analysis 41, 549–559.
van Kollenburg GH, Mulder J and Vermunt JK (2015) Assessing model fit in latent class analysis when asymptotics do not hold. Methodology 11, 65–79.
Dendukuri N, Hadgu A and Wang L (2009) Modeling conditional dependence between diagnostic tests: a multiple latent variable model. Statistics in Medicine 28, 441–461.
Xu H and Craig BA (2009) A probit latent class model with general correlation structures for evaluating accuracy of diagnostic tests. Biometrics 65, 1145–1155.

Habib I et al. (2008) A Bayesian modelling framework to estimate Campylobacter prevalence and culture methods sensitivity: application to a chicken meat survey in Belgium. Journal of Applied Microbiology 105, 2002–2008.
Regula G et al. (2005) Evaluation of an antimicrobial resistance monitoring program for campylobacter in poultry by simulation. Preventive Veterinary Medicine 70, 29–43.

Freydiere AM et al. New methods for detection of campylobacters in 319 paediatric stool samples: comparison of two immunochromatographic tests with culture, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) and polymerase chain reaction (PCR) methods [abstract]. Presented at the 23th European Congress of Clinical Microbiology and Infectious Diseases, Berlin, Germany, April 27–30, 2013.

Chen JH and Hung WL (2015) An automatic clustering algorithm for probability density functions. Journal of Statistical Computation and Simulation 85, 3047–3063.
Van Smeden M et al. (2016) Problems in detecting misfit of latent class models in diagnostic research without a gold standard were shown. Journal of Clinical Epidemiology 74, 158–166.

Collins J and Albert PS (2016) Estimating diagnostic accuracy without a gold standard: a continued controversy. Journal of Biopharmaceutical Statistics 26, 1078–1082.
Schiller I et al. (2016) Bias due to composite reference standards in diagnostic accuracy studies. Statistics in Medicine 35, 1454–1470.
Hadgu A, Dendukuri N and Wang L (2012) Evaluation of screening tests for detecting Chlamydia trachomatis: bias associated with the patient-infected-status algorithm. Epidemiology 23, 72–82.
